So there's four levels here based on the temperature of the customer through your funnel. Cold customers are people that have not engaged and not clicked in any ad. So for all intents and purposes, they don't know who you are. Then the warm layer is people that have engaged but not clicked. So people that engage may have viewed a video may have subscribed to an email list and you know bypass your website completely. Or they could be just people that are fans, for example. Then for me, the definition of hot is someone that's clicked onto your website and of course there'll be different temperature gradings based on how far down your particular funnel they've got. So someone that's visited a blog page is a different level of quality to someone that's visited your product page or your cart page. And then existing customers are people that have already purchased and then how you handle those is then going to drive your lifetime value loyalty referral and things like that. testing is going to give you greater success. So my approach is not to just fire up hundreds of different ads and hundreds of different ad sets and see what works. So the way I like to set up my funnels is to really simplify that the top of the funnel is cold, and then exclude the level below. ## [09:08 - 12:09]

be that the consideration phase of your products is a bit higher. But you know, let's say for example, you're selling a $50 or $100 Econ products. If you haven't convinced them with the first click and the first visit to the site, then you need to think back and say, right, is it that someone needs to just think about it? In which case, I'll just serve up some retargeting ads as reminder. Or have I actually missed something in my landing page, and therefore I need to deliver it in my retargeting message. So for example, if let's say for example, you're selling apparel, the problem with selling apparel online is that you can't check the fit properly. Now if you can't check the fit, can you send it back? We had a client that in our retargeting ads, we didn't do so much in the product page, in our retargeting ads, we were making it clear as one of the messages that you can return it for free within 30 days. When we actually shifted to have that on the product page, the average order value actually went up. Now why is this? The reason was that people were buying more than they otherwise would, because they were comforted by the fact that you could return it back for free within 30 days. And we did see an uptick in returns, but the net positive and profitability in ROAS, considering the cost of returns and everything else was far greater than not having it. So, you know, the barriers for not buying are both for your retargeting page and also your product page to think about how you can convert them. So these are all kind of conversion metrics and kind of thinking about how we can get someone back into our funnel and buy. Then into existing customers, you know, this for me is money left on the table if you're not retargeting existing customers. So it could be doing a post purchase sequence right after the purchase. It could be bringing people in that have not purchased for a while. And you know, you know, for example, people buy every few months, it could be lapses, lapses of people that are out of that kind of process, and they're not buying again. It could be retargeting your VIP customers, it could be targeting your high value customers or different things. One of the things I look at is RFM, recency, frequency and monetary value. Recency is how recent have they bought. Frequency is how frequently have they bought and monetary value is essentially how much of the money have they spent with you. And when you start to model that for your existing customers, you'll get an idea for who are your VIPs, who actually buys more frequently, who are your new customers, and you can segment them and target them differently. I would always combine email with Facebook ads, primarily emails better because obviously you've got the customer, you don't have to pay for them again.
